findings.summary: TYPE_CHECKING block in manuscript_overview.py references non-existent module executive_report (missing trailing 'er')
evidence: Line 23: from infrastructure.reporting.executive_report import ExecutiveSummary
Actual module on disk is infrastructure/reporting/executive_reporter.py — no executive_report.py exists
This import is guarded by TYPE_CHECKING so it does not fail at runtime, but it is a dead/wrong reference that will break any static analysis tool (mypy) that follows it
findings.suggestion: Change the import to 'from infrastructure.reporting.executive_reporter import ExecutiveSummary'

findings.summary: llm/review/generator.py fetches TRANSLATION_LANGUAGES from pipeline_runner, which itself re-imports it from templates.manuscript — a needless indirection through a higher-level module
evidence: generator.py line 740: from infrastructure.llm.review.pipeline_runner import TRANSLATION_LANGUAGES
pipeline_runner.py line 16: from infrastructure.llm.templates.manuscript import TRANSLATION_LANGUAGES
The constant is defined in templates/manuscript.py; generator.py should import it directly from there, not via pipeline_runner, which is a peer/higher orchestration module
findings.suggestion: In generator.py replace the deferred import with a direct top-level import: 'from infrastructure.llm.templates.manuscript import TRANSLATION_LANGUAGES'

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/cli.py imports MultiProjectOrchestrator and PipelineSummaryGenerator, blending pipeline orchestration into what should be a thin core CLI entry point
evidence: cli.py lines 32-34 import MultiProjectConfig, MultiProjectOrchestrator, PipelineConfig, PipelineExecutor, and PipelineSummaryGenerator all at top-level
This makes core/cli.py a de facto orchestrator rather than a thin dispatcher, tightly coupling the core CLI to two other submodules of core/
findings.suggestion: Move orchestration imports inside the handler functions (lazy imports) or extract the orchestration logic into a dedicated scripts/ entry point, leaving cli.py as a pure argument-parser facade

findings.summary: coverage_parser.py docstring explicitly states it is an internal module not re-exported, yet its symbols are used directly by test_runner.py and scripts/01_run_tests.py without going through the __init__.py surface — the boundary intent is documented but not enforced
evidence: coverage_parser.py line 6: 'Internal module: import directly (not re-exported from infrastructure.reporting).'
test_runner.py line 21: from infrastructure.reporting.coverage_parser import ...
scripts/01_run_tests.py line 39: from infrastructure.reporting.coverage_parser import check_cov_datafile_support

The module is not listed in reporting/__init__.py __all__, but external callers still bypass the public surface and import it directly — the 'internal' label is advisory only, not structurally enforced
findings.suggestion: Either add coverage_parser's public API to reporting/__init__.py and remove the 'internal' disclaimer, or enforce the boundary by marking the file with a leading underscore (_coverage_parser.py) and fixing all import sites

findings.summary: Logging functionality is split across logging_utils.py, logging_constants.py, logging_formatters.py, logging_helpers.py, and logging_progress.py — five files for one cross-cutting concern, creating unnecessary navigation overhead
evidence: All five files live at the same level in infrastructure/core/ and serve the single concern of logging
scripts/01_run_tests.py imports from both logging_utils and logging_helpers in the same file (lines 28 and 37)
The public __init__.py only re-exports from logging_utils, leaving the other four as undiscoverable implementation details
findings.suggestion: Consolidate into a single infrastructure/core/logging/ sub-package with a clean __init__.py, or at minimum reduce to two files: logging_utils.py (primary API) and logging_internals.py (formatters, constants)

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/performance.py is documented as a backward-compatibility shim that purely re-exports from stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py, adding an indirection layer without any logic
evidence: performance.py lines 14-28: entire file is re-exports from the two split modules
Docstring says: 'Import from those modules directly for new code. This shim exists so that existing call sites continue to work'
The shim perpetuates two canonical locations for the same symbols, which misleads future contributors about which module is authoritative
findings.suggestion: Migrate remaining call sites to import directly from stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py, then delete performance.py

findings.summary: infrastructure/steganography/ is a self-contained domain feature (QR codes, barcodes, overlays, encryption) placed alongside generic build infrastructure, which inflates the infrastructure surface and creates a misleading 'everything is infrastructure' pattern
evidence: Directory contains 5 files covering encryption, barcode generation, QR codes, and PDF overlays — none of these are generic build/validation utilities
pyproject.toml lists 'steganography' as an optional dependency group with qrcode[pil] and python-barcode, signalling it is a separable feature
No other infrastructure module imports from infrastructure.steganography, making it an isolated island
findings.suggestion: Move to a top-level features/steganography/ or projects/steganography/ directory, or clearly document it as an optional plugin module separate from core infrastructure

findings.summary: pyproject.toml default-groups includes 'monitoring' (psutil), meaning every developer install gets psutil even though the code treats it as optional (gracefully absent)
evidence: pyproject.toml line 101: default-groups = ["dev", "monitoring"]
infrastructure/core/_optional_deps.py and health_check.py wrap psutil in try/except and handle its absence gracefully
Declaring psutil as a default group defeats the optional/graceful-absent design and adds an unnecessary C-extension dependency to all developer environments
findings.suggestion: Remove 'monitoring' from default-groups; leave it as an opt-in group. The graceful-absence wrappers already handle the psutil-unavailable case correctly.

findings.summary: dashboard_generator.py is over 3000 lines and conditionally imports plotly and matplotlib inside functions, creating a large optional module that is difficult to test and maintain as a single file
evidence: reporting/__init__.py wraps the entire dashboard_generator import in try/except ImportError (lines 48-57)

dashboard_generator.py lines 522, 820, 3162: repeated deferred local imports of calculate_project_health_score from executive_reporter within function bodies, suggesting the file grew organically without refactoring
A 3000+ line file with scattered internal imports is a coupling and maintenance liability regardless of its optional status
findings.suggestion: Split dashboard_generator.py into dashboard_matplotlib.py and dashboard_plotly.py (matching the existing generate_matplotlib_dashboard / generate_plotly_dashboard split already exported), each with its own optional import guard

findings.summary: scientific/templates.py raises generic ValueError/TypeError instead of the project's custom exception hierarchy
evidence: templates.py line 68: raise TypeError(f'param1 must be numeric...')
templates.py line 70: raise TypeError(f'param2 must be integer...')
templates.py lines 77, 98, 101: raise ValueError(...)
infrastructure/__init__.py line 34: raise RuntimeError(...) — another built-in exception used at the top-level shim
The project defines DataValidationError, ConfigurationError, BuildError etc. in exceptions.py but templates.py ignores all of them
findings.suggestion: Replace bare ValueError/TypeError raises in templates.py with the appropriate custom exceptions (DataValidationError, InvalidConfigurationError, etc.) so that callers can catch TemplateError broadly as intended by the hierarchy.

findings.summary: 174 occurrences of bare 'except Exception' across 57 modules, many silently return None/False or log at debug/warning without re-raising
evidence: health_check.py line 55: except Exception as e — catches all check failures, logs warning, continues; never raises
pipeline.py _execute_stage line 253: except Exception as e — converts all exceptions to a failed stage result; custom exceptions from exceptions.py are indistinguishable from IOError
generator.py line 465: except Exception as e — warmup failure returns (False, 0.0); caller cannot distinguish timeout from OOM from network error
generator.py line 640: except Exception as e — per-attempt failure logs at debug and continues, swallowing error type

Modules that DO narrow exceptions: coverage_parser.py catches (json.JSONDecodeError, OSError) specifically; integrity.py catches (OSError, ValueError, pickle.UnpicklingError) specifically — creating two distinct patterns in the same codebase
findings.suggestion: Narrow broad except-Exception clauses to the specific exceptions expected (OSError, subprocess.SubprocessError, etc.) and let unexpected exceptions propagate. Where swallowing is intentional (health checks), document it explicitly. Use chain_exceptions() from exceptions.py to attach context before re-raising.

findings.summary: Logging calls mix f-string interpolation and %-style formatting in the same codebase, and even within the same module
evidence: pipeline.py line 142: logger.info(f'Executing full pipeline...') — f-string
pipeline.py line 175: logger.error(PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED.format(...)) — .format() via error constant
pipeline_reporter.py line 124: logger.error('Failed to write JSON report %s: %s', json_path, e) — %-style
pipeline_reporter.py line 133: logger.info(f'Pipeline report (JSON) saved: {json_path}') — f-string in same file
503 f-string logger calls vs 43 %-style calls across infrastructure/ — both styles exist at scale
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one logging style. The project's existing dominance is f-strings (503 vs 43), so convert the remaining %-style calls to f-strings, or vice versa if lazy evaluation is preferred. Pick one and enforce via a linter rule (e.g., G004 in flake8-logging-format).

findings.summary: suite_summary_generator.py places TypedDict class definition before stdlib/infrastructure imports, breaking PEP 8 import ordering
evidence: Lines 15-27: class InfraResults(TypedDict) is defined between the stdlib import block (json, datetime, pathlib, typing) and the infrastructure imports (config_loader, logging_utils)
All other modules in the codebase follow the standard pattern: stdlib imports, then third-party, then local — no class definitions interleaved
This is the only file across the reviewed set where a type definition appears inside the import section
findings.suggestion: Move InfraResults TypedDict to after all imports, consistent with every other module in the codebase.

findings.summary: generator.py omits the required two blank lines before several top-level function definitions, unlike all other reviewed modules
evidence: Line 66: def get_manuscript_review_system_prompt() immediately follows the try/except PROMPT_SYSTEM_AVAILABLE block with no blank line separator
Lines 95-96: def log_timeout_info() follows the closing triple-quote of the inline string literal with only one blank line
All other infrastructure modules (pipeline.py, health_check.py, coverage_parser.py, integrity.py) consistently use two blank lines between top-level definitions
findings.suggestion: Add the missing two-blank-line separators around top-level function definitions in generator.py to match PEP 8 and the rest of the codebase.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py has two bare expression statements (infra_results.get('total', 0)) that compute a value and discard it, a pattern not seen elsewhere
evidence: Line 324: infra_results.get('total', 0) — result assigned to nothing; the value is never used
Line 423: project_results.get('total', 0) — same pattern
All other .get() calls in the same function (passed, failed, skipped, coverage) are assigned to local variables and then used in log messages
findings.suggestion: Either assign the result to a local variable that is used (e.g., infra_total = infra_results.get('total', 0)) or remove the dead call entirely.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py implements substantial failure-analysis logic (error-type detection, targeted fix suggestions) that belongs in an infrastructure module
evidence: Lines 377-415: report_results() contains if/elif chains that classify error types (import errors, coverage errors, timeout errors) by string-searching failure dicts and emit tailored advice — this is business logic, not orchestration

Lines 459-526: the same classification block is copy-pasted verbatim for the project test branch (has_import_errors, has_assertion_errors, has_coverage_errors, has_timeout_errors checks with identical logger.info calls)
The project's CLAUDE.md explicitly forbids logic in scripts: 'Scripts are lightweight coordinators that... Never implement algorithms or business logic'
The run_infrastructure_tests() and run_project_tests() functions also have 80+ lines of command assembly that crosses into decision logic rather than delegation
findings.suggestion: Extract the failure classification and suggestion generation into a function in infrastructure/reporting/ (e.g., format_failure_suggestions(failed_tests, test_suite) -> list[str]), and deduplicate the two identical blocks. Keep the script as a thin caller.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.generate_validation_report() writes files without try/except, inconsistent with save_pipeline_report() in the same module which wraps all writes
evidence: generate_validation_report() lines 447-458: open() and write_text() calls are unwrapped — any OSError propagates uncaught to callers
save_pipeline_report() lines 116-151: every file write is wrapped in try/except OSError with logger.error() + raise
save_error_summary() lines 511-519: also unwrapped writes
generate_performance_report() lines 486-489: also unwrapped write
Only the three functions added in the original pipeline_reporter design have error handling; the utility helpers added later do not
findings.suggestion: Apply the same try/except OSError + logger.error + raise pattern used in save_pipeline_report() to generate_validation_report(), save_error_summary(), and generate_performance_report() for uniform error surface.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/performance.py re-exports symbols from two different sub-modules (stage_monitor, function_profiler) without distinguishing their concerns, leaving callers unable to import coherently
evidence: performance.py re-exports PerformanceMonitor (pipeline stage resource tracking) alongside CodeProfiler (cProfile/tracemalloc function profiling) — these are conceptually distinct tools
The shim's own docstring admits the split ('two sub-domains have been split into dedicated modules') yet keeps them co-exported, perpetuating the confusion

tests/infra_tests/core/test_performance.py imports directly from stage_monitor and function_profiler (lines 11-23), not from performance.py — indicating the shim is no longer the canonical import path even within the test suite
findings.suggestion: Deprecate or remove the performance.py shim and update all remaining call sites to import from stage_monitor or function_profiler directly. If backward compatibility is required, keep the shim but add a deprecation warning on import.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/performance.py is a pure re-export shim with no value-add
evidence: Lines 1-44: entire file is `from infrastructure.core.function_profiler import ...` and `from infrastructure.core.stage_monitor import ...` with an `__all__` list
The module docstring acknowledges it exists only for backward compatibility ('Import from those modules directly for new code')
The shim exports 11 names from two sub-modules — all call sites could import from the real modules directly
findings.suggestion: Migrate all call sites to import directly from stage_monitor or function_profiler, then delete the shim. If backward compatibility is required during a transition, pin the deadline in a TODO comment with a ticket reference.

findings.summary: StageSpec.skip flag mixes stage description with execution policy, leaking control flow into data
evidence: Line 65-70: `class StageSpec(NamedTuple)` carries a `skip: bool = False` field
Lines 182-200: `_execute_pipeline` iterates stages and branches on `stage_spec.skip` to `continue`

The skip condition is evaluated at stage construction time (_build_stage_list lines 119-133), so a stage can never dynamically decide to run; the flag is set from config booleans at build time, not at execution time
This means the stage list describes both what stages exist and which to suppress — two separate concerns
findings.suggestion: Filter skipped stages out of the list before passing to `_execute_pipeline`, or introduce a separate `active_stages` vs `all_stages` concept. `StageSpec` should only describe a stage, not encode execution policy.

findings.summary: Module-level mutable singleton `_checker` in health_check.py is hidden global state
evidence: Lines 278-286: `_checker: SystemHealthChecker | None = None` and `get_health_checker()` implements a lazy global singleton
The singleton captures `_PROCESS_START_TIME` at import time (line 277), making tests that import this module time-dependent
No mechanism to reset the singleton between test runs, so repeated calls return the same stale object with a fixed start time
findings.suggestion: Remove the module-level singleton. Callers that need a shared checker should manage lifetime themselves, or pass `start_time` explicitly. For tests, the class can be instantiated directly.

findings.summary: Exception constructors implement OS detection and install-command generation — business logic misplaced in exception classes
evidence: Lines 243-268: `_build_install_commands()` contains platform detection with `platform.system()` and `shutil.which()` for four package managers
Lines 283-314: `MissingDependencyError.__init__` calls `_build_install_commands` and generates suggestion strings
Lines 182-219: `FileNotFoundError.__init__` generates `ls -la` and `find` shell commands
Exception constructors are responsible for OS-specific install heuristics — this makes exception construction side-effectful and hard to test in isolation
findings.suggestion: Move install-command generation to a dedicated helper in a diagnostics or environment module. Exception classes should only carry data; the caller or a display layer should enrich with recovery suggestions.

findings.summary: suite_summary_generator.py uses relative path literals instead of accepting a repo_root parameter
evidence: Line 37: `results_file = Path(f'projects/{project_name}/output/reports/test_results.json')` — relative path, cwd-dependent
Line 75: `infra_results_file = Path('infrastructure_validation_report.json')` — relative path
Line 100: `for coverage_path in (Path('coverage_infra.json'), Path('htmlcov/coverage.json'))` — relative paths
Lines 120-130: `discover_active_projects` uses `Path('projects')` — relative path
`generate_summary_report()` takes no arguments and is entirely cwd-dependent; tests use `monkeypatch.chdir` to work around this
findings.suggestion: Add a `repo_root: Path` parameter to `generate_summary_report`, `load_test_results`, `load_infrastructure_results`, and `discover_active_projects`. Derive all paths from it. Remove the `monkeypatch.chdir` workaround from tests.

findings.summary: Nearly-empty exception subclasses carry disproportionately long docstrings with fabricated examples
evidence: Lines 79-83: `ConfigurationError` has a one-line docstring for a `pass` body
Lines 85-88: `MissingConfigurationError` docstring string ends without a period — minor but indicates template paste
Lines 270-280: `MissingDependencyError` docstring includes a multi-line `>>> raise ...` example that only illustrates constructor arguments already visible in the signature
Lines 317-327: `VersionMismatchError` docstring example block is copy-pasted structure from `MissingDependencyError` with only context dict values changed
findings.suggestion: Strip examples from exception subclass docstrings. A one-line description is sufficient for exception subclasses. Examples belong in developer docs, not inline docstrings on raise-only classes.

findings.summary: __init__ docstring restates internal implementation details that belong in inline comments
evidence: Lines 77-89: `__init__` docstring describes that it 'Sets up per-project log file that captures all pipeline execution logs (Python + subprocess output). Log file is located at: projects/{project_name}/output/logs/pipeline.log' — this is implementation detail, not interface documentation
Lines 91-99: Inline comments explain `log_dir`, `log_file`, `self.log_file`, and `self._log_handler` separately, repeating what the code already states
Line 97: comment '# Set up log file handler initially' is followed immediately by `self._setup_log_file_handler()` — the comment adds no information
findings.suggestion: Remove the implementation detail paragraph from `__init__` docstring; keep only the Args/Returns interface contract. Remove comments that restate what the adjacent code line does.

findings.summary: pipeline_reporter.py uses comment banners to mark sections whose purpose is already clear from the code
evidence: Lines 115-116: `# Generate JSON report` immediately before `if 'json' in formats:`
Lines 127-128: `# Generate Markdown report` immediately before `if 'markdown' in formats:`
Lines 139-140: `# Generate HTML report` immediately before `if 'html' in formats:`
Lines 102-103: `# Convert to dictionary for serialization` before a dict literal — comment restates the line
Lines 359-360: `# Convert to checkpoint format` in `_save_checkpoint` before a list comprehension that is clearly building checkpoint format
findings.suggestion: Remove comments that restate what the immediately following code statement already expresses. Reserve inline comments for non-obvious decisions (e.g., why exit code 2 is treated as success).

findings.summary: Private methods in SystemHealthChecker have docstrings describing obvious behavior
evidence: Line 77: `def _check_filesystem(self) -> dict[str, Any]:` has docstring 'Check filesystem health and permissions.' — restates the method name
Line 252: `def _generate_summary(self, status)` has no docstring at all while public methods do — inconsistent
Lines 110-121: `_check_memory`, `_check_cpu`, `_check_disk` have no docstrings at all despite the public `get_health_status` having one — pattern inconsistency from likely partial generation
findings.suggestion: Apply a consistent docstring policy: either all private methods get one-line docstrings, or none do. Do not mix verbose docstrings on trivial methods with absent docstrings on equally trivial adjacent methods.

findings.summary: Several tests in test_environment.py assert only on types rather than values, providing near-zero failure signal
evidence: Lines 35-42: `test_check_default_dependencies` asserts only `isinstance(all_present, bool)` and `isinstance(missing, list)` — these can never fail unless the function raises
Lines 75-80: `test_returns_tuple` asserts `isinstance(result, tuple)` and `len(result) == 2` — zero behavioral signal

Lines 111-119: `TestEnvironmentState.test_import_system_working` checks that the module has attributes named `check_python_version` and `check_dependencies` and that they are callable — this would pass even if both functions always raised NotImplementedError
findings.suggestion: Replace type-only assertions with value assertions. `test_check_default_dependencies` should check that known-present packages are not in `missing`. `test_returns_tuple` should be deleted — it tests Python's type system, not the function.

findings.summary: TestGenerateMultiProjectSummaryReport uses locally-defined mock dataclasses that duplicate production types
evidence: Lines 343-386: `test_generate_with_successful_projects` defines `MockProject`, `MockStageResult`, and `MockResult` dataclasses inline — three structural dummies that mirror production types
Lines 399-443: `test_generate_with_failed_projects` redefines the same three mock dataclasses from scratch with minor variation
Lines 445-475, 477-521, 522-562, 564-591: each test method in the class redefines its own version of these same mock types — six separate inline class definitions total
This pattern defeats duck-typing benefits and will silently pass if production code changes its expected interface
findings.suggestion: Define shared test fixtures at module scope (or a conftest) that use the real production types. If production types cannot be used directly, define mock dataclasses once at module scope. Inline redefinition per test is a maintenance and correctness hazard.

findings.summary: StagePerformanceTracker tests bypass the public API and directly set internal state
evidence: Lines 404-423: `test_get_performance_warnings_slow_stage` directly assigns `tracker.stages = [...]` instead of calling `start_stage` / `end_stage`
Lines 431-447: `test_get_performance_warnings_high_memory` directly assigns `tracker.stages = [...]`
Lines 449-465: `test_get_performance_warnings_high_cpu` directly assigns `tracker.stages = [...]`
Lines 467-500: `test_get_summary` directly assigns `tracker.stages = [...]`
These tests validate the warning/summary computation but do not verify that the computation is reachable via the real `start_stage`/`end_stage` path, which is the only path users have
findings.suggestion: Use `start_stage` / `end_stage` with controlled sleep values to produce real stage data. If performance warning thresholds make this awkward in tests, extract the threshold constants and pass them as parameters so tests can set low thresholds.

findings.summary: test_execute_full_pipeline_success hardcodes stage count and name list, making it brittle to pipeline evolution
evidence: Lines 262-280: asserts `len(results) == 9` and checks an exact ordered list of nine stage names
Line 306: `test_execute_core_pipeline_success` asserts `len(results) == 7` and checks exact names
Any addition or reordering of a pipeline stage requires updating multiple test assertions — the test tests structure rather than behavior
findings.suggestion: Assert on behavioral properties: all results succeeded, required stages are present (set membership not ordered list), pipeline was not aborted early. Remove exact count and exact order assertions; those belong in a dedicated 'stage ordering contract' test that is clearly labeled as such.

findings.summary: test_check_cov_datafile_support only asserts the return type is bool, not whether detection is correct
evidence: Lines 164-166: `test_returns_bool` calls `check_cov_datafile_support()` and asserts `isinstance(result, bool)` — this would pass even if the function always returned `False` or always raised and was caught internally
The function makes a real subprocess call to `pytest --help`; the test could assert that when pytest is installed the result is `True`, which is a real behavioral claim
findings.suggestion: Assert that `check_cov_datafile_support()` returns `True` in the test environment (pytest-cov is a declared test dependency). A False result in CI would be a real signal of a missing dependency.

findings.summary: scripts/01_run_tests.py contains substantial non-orchestration logic rather than delegating to infrastructure
evidence: report_results() at line 276 is 330+ lines of inline logging, conditional branching, error-type detection, and suggestion generation — all business logic that belongs in infrastructure/reporting/

run_infrastructure_tests() and run_project_tests() build complex pytest command lists (lines 108-135, 201-239), manage PYTHONPATH, check uv availability, and call check_cov_datafile_support directly — these are not orchestration concerns
Lines 377-415 and 476-526: duplicated error-type detection blocks (has_import_errors, has_coverage_errors, has_timeout_errors) appear nearly verbatim in both the infra and project failure paths
findings.suggestion: Extract report_results() and the command-building logic into infrastructure/reporting/ modules. The script should call one function per phase and let the module handle display logic.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/performance.py is a pure re-export shim with no logic, indicating a split that was not fully resolved
evidence: The entire 44-line file is imports and __all__ — it exists only because callers still import from performance.py after the module was split into stage_monitor.py and function_profiler.py
The docstring admits this is a 'backward-compatible re-export shim', which is a design smell: either callers should have been migrated or the split should not have happened
findings.suggestion: Either migrate all call sites to import from the canonical sub-modules and delete the shim, or keep performance.py as the canonical module and inline the sub-module content back into it.

findings.summary: IntegrityReport uses a hand-rolled __init__ with plain list/dict assignments instead of a dataclass
evidence: Lines 28-39: IntegrityReport.__init__ manually assigns seven attributes that are all straightforward typed fields; no custom logic justifies avoiding @dataclass
The class has no methods beyond construction — it is a plain data container
findings.suggestion: Convert IntegrityReport to a @dataclass with field() defaults for the mutable list/dict attributes.

findings.summary: validate_review_quality() is a 200-line conditional dispatch function that handles five structurally different review types in one body
evidence: Lines 104-313: a single function with five elif branches (executive_summary, quality_review, methodology_review, improvement_suggestions, translation), each with different local variables, different pattern lists, and different issue strings
Each branch is effectively an independent validator; they share only the off-topic and word-count checks at the top
findings.suggestion: Extract each branch into a dedicated private function (e.g., _validate_executive_summary, _validate_quality_review, etc.) dispatched by a dict. The shared top-level checks remain in validate_review_quality().

findings.summary: report_results() in 01_run_tests.py duplicates the failure-reporting block nearly verbatim for infrastructure and project suites
evidence: Lines 348-415 (infrastructure failure path) and lines 447-526 (project failure path) are structurally identical: same shape of failed_tests iteration, same error-type detection variables, same suggestion strings
The only differences are the variable names (infra_results vs project_results) and one added has_assertion_errors check in the project path
findings.suggestion: Extract a _report_suite_failure(suite_name, results, project_name) helper and call it from both failure branches.

findings.summary: get_metrics() repeats the same three-level dict-walk pattern for memory, cpu, and disk without abstraction
evidence: Lines 235-248: three nearly identical blocks each doing status['checks'][X].get('details', {}).get('Y') and assigning to metrics[Z] — the only variation is the key names
findings.suggestion: Extract a helper _extract_check_metric(status, check_name, detail_key) and call it three times.

findings.summary: any() is called with a tuple literal instead of a generator expression, making it always True for non-empty tuples
evidence: Lines 211-219: has_assessment = any(("clarity" in response_lower, "structure" in response_lower, ...)) — this passes a tuple of booleans to any(), which is correct in result but misleading; if the intent were short-circuit evaluation, a generator expression is required
The same pattern appears at lines 241-249, 271-279, and 286-293 — four occurrences total

any((True, False, False)) correctly returns True, but any((False, False, False)) also works correctly — however the style is non-idiomatic and the tuple is fully evaluated before any() sees it, defeating any short-circuit benefit
findings.suggestion: Replace any((expr1, expr2, ...)) with any([expr1, expr2, ...]) or the idiomatic expr1 or expr2 or ... for simple boolean chains.

findings.summary: Two .get('total', 0) calls in report_results() are assigned to a variable that is immediately discarded
evidence: Line 324: infra_results.get("total", 0) — the return value is not assigned to anything; the line is a no-op
Line 423: project_results.get("total", 0) — same pattern, return value discarded
findings.suggestion: Assign to total = infra_results.get("total", 0) and use the variable, or remove the line if it was meant to be used elsewhere.

findings.summary: generate_integrity_report() embeds emoji characters in the returned string, making the output fragile in non-UTF-8 terminals and harder to parse
evidence: Lines 363-419: report strings contain emoji such as 'INTEGRITY VERIFICATION REPORT', 'PASSED', 'FAILED', 'File Integrity:', etc. — these are baked into a programmatic string-builder function, not a display layer
findings.suggestion: Separate the emoji/decoration layer from the data assembly. Either move emoji to a display-layer caller or use plain ASCII symbols (PASS/FAIL) in the returned string.

findings.summary: Logging calls throughout the codebase mix f-strings (evaluated eagerly) with %-format strings inconsistently
evidence: pipeline.py line 142: logger.info(f"Executing full pipeline...") — f-string
pipeline.py line 176: logger.error(PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED.format(...)) — str.format
health_check.py line 56: logger.warning(f"Health check failed for {check_name}: {e}") — f-string
integrity.py line 77: logger.error("Error verifying %s: %s", file_path, e, exc_info=True) — %-format
No single convention is followed; %-format is preferred for logging because it defers string interpolation
findings.suggestion: Adopt %-format strings as the project-wide logging convention and apply it consistently. Reserve f-strings for non-logging contexts.

findings.summary: create_integrity_manifest() uses unnecessary local typed-alias variables to work around mypy complaints on a dict[str, Any]
evidence: Lines 623-626: file_hashes: dict[str, Any] = manifest["file_hashes"] and dir_structure: dict[str, Any] = manifest["directory_structure"] exist solely to give mypy narrower types on a dict that was already typed as dict[str, Any]
The manifest dict is mutated through both the aliases and direct access to manifest["file_count"] / manifest["total_size"] at lines 643-644, creating two different access patterns for the same object
findings.suggestion: Use a TypedDict or dataclass for the manifest return type so all fields are typed at the source, eliminating the need for alias workarounds.
